passage: Researchers studied 1,200 adults aged 40–65 and recorded their serum vitamin D levels (in ng/mL) alongside bone mineral density (BMD) scores. Participants with vitamin D levels below 12 ng/mL had an average BMD score of 0.71 g/cm². Those with levels of 12–20 ng/mL had an average score of 0.79 g/cm². Participants with levels of 20–30 ng/mL had an average score of 0.88 g/cm², and those with levels above 30 ng/mL had an average score of 0.94 g/cm². The researchers noted that participants who exercised more than three times per week had higher BMD scores within every vitamin D category.
question: A physician claims that vitamin D supplementation, which raises serum vitamin D levels, is likely to improve bone mineral density. Which finding from the study most directly supports this claim?

choices.A: Participants who exercised more than three times per week had higher BMD scores within every vitamin D category.
choices.B: Participants with vitamin D levels above 30 ng/mL had the highest average BMD scores in the study at 0.94 g/cm².
choices.C: BMD scores increased progressively with each higher vitamin D level category, from 0.71 g/cm² for the lowest category to 0.94 g/cm² for the highest.
choices.D: The study enrolled 1,200 adults between the ages of 40 and 65.

explanation: The physician's claim rests on a positive relationship between higher vitamin D levels and better bone density. C describes the complete monotonic increase in BMD across all four vitamin D categories, establishing a consistent association that directly supports the claim. B identifies only the highest group's result and cannot alone establish the broader relationship. A introduces exercise as a separate variable and says nothing specific about the vitamin D–BMD relationship. D is a sample description with no relevance to the claim.
